claim: AGENTS.md is positioned as a predictable README-like place for coding-agent context and project instructions, not a replacement for human README files.
AGENTS.md examples include setup commands, code style, testing instructions, and PR instructions; this supports using it as an agent harness entrypoint.
AGENTS.md instructions should be living documentation and closer files win when instructions conflict.
Claude Code treats CLAUDE.md files as persistent instructions loaded into context; they are context, not enforced configuration, so concise structured instructions improve adherence.
Claude recommends adding durable instructions when the assistant repeats a mistake, code review catches something it should know, or a new teammate would need the same context.
Skills are configured with YAML frontmatter and markdown content; they can encode reusable domain knowledge, conventions, patterns, and workflows.
Skills can restrict activation by paths and pre-approve allowed tools, making them suitable as scoped behavior modules rather than global prompts.
Subagents run with their own context window, custom system prompt, tool access, and independent permissions; they are useful when repeated side tasks would flood the main conversation.
Skills can be preloaded into subagents, but tool access and Skill availability are still controlled separately.
Hooks execute at defined lifecycle events such as PreToolUse, PostToolUse, InstructionsLoaded, ConfigChange and FileChanged, giving a deterministic layer around model behavior.
PreToolUse hooks can block unsafe operations and PostToolUse hooks can run checks such as linters after edits.
Anthropic recommends the simplest viable agent design, transparent planning, and thorough tool documentation/testing; complexity should be added only when it improves outcomes.
Coding agents are valuable when output quality can be objectively measured, but human review remains crucial beyond automated tests.
MCP standardizes connecting AI apps to external systems such as data sources, tools, and workflows; harness design must govern these connections.

OWASP LLM risks directly map to agent harness concerns: prompt injection, insecure output handling, supply chain vulnerabilities, sensitive information disclosure, insecure plugin design, excessive agency, and overreliance.
Promptfoo positions evals as testing prompts, models, and RAG pipelines; this supports treating prompt/context/harness changes as testable artifacts.
SWE-bench frames coding-agent evaluation as resolving real-world GitHub issues and notes containerized evaluation for reproducibility.
Windsurf also separates persistent context into memories, rules, workflows, skills and AGENTS.md; this confirms the ecosystem pattern of multiple context layers that can drift.
GitHub Copilot supports repository custom instructions, showing that repo-level agent guidance is now a cross-tool pattern, not just AGENTS.md.
GitHub warns against conflicting custom instruction sets; this supports the article’s alignment-drift framing.
Claude Code best practices recommend giving Claude a way to verify work and including Bash commands, code style, and workflow rules in CLAUDE.md.
Claude Code best practices recommend explore-first, plan-then-code workflows and active course correction; this maps to harness instructions and eval cases.
Claude Code permissions provide fine-grained control over what the agent can access and do, including tool-specific permission rules.
Additional directories may grant file access without loading configuration by default; this is a concrete example of context/tool scope being different from configuration scope.
Windsurf has a SKILL.md file format, confirming that skills as reusable workflow/context modules are an ecosystem pattern.
Windsurf workflows are another explicit workflow layer, reinforcing the need to align AGENTS.md, rules, skills, and workflows.
Gemini CLI uses hierarchical memory loaded from GEMINI.md files and can inspect/reload that instructional context.
Aider supports convention files read into chat, showing another implementation of project-specific coding-agent guidance.
OpenCode agents are specialized assistants configured for specific tasks/workflows with custom prompts, models, and tool access.
OpenCode distinguishes primary agents and subagents; the plan agent can analyze/review without code changes, a useful pattern for safe review skills.
Promptfoo includes agent trajectory assertions such as tool-used, tool-args-match, tool-sequence and step-count; this directly supports harness evals for agent behavior, not only final text.
Promptfoo red teaming frames AI apps as nondeterministic and security-sensitive; this supports testing adversarial harness failures like prompt injection and excessive agency.
NIST AI RMF provides a risk-management frame for AI systems; for coding-agent harnesses this supports risk-tiered permissions/evals.
GitHub’s Copilot guidance emphasizes providing context such as workspace awareness and selected code; context engineering is a practical developer action.
